commonIssues: Prefers cool summers; air temperatures above ~29 C (85 F) can damage plants and intense sun can scorch leaves, so afternoon part-shade is helpful in hot climates.
Susceptible to powdery mildew, anthracnose and fungal leaf spot in humid conditions or with poor air circulation.
Common pests include currant aphids, imported currant worms, scale, spider mites, cane borers and fruit flies.
Currants are an alternate host for white pine blister rust; planting is restricted or banned in some U.S. jurisdictions.
Shallow-rooted, so keep soil consistently moist (about 1 to 2 inches of water per week) and mulch; container plants dry out faster and need more frequent watering.
Container minimum is a realistic home-garden pot for this 3 to 5 ft bush (RHS advises at least a 45 cm / 18 in wide pot, roughly 55 to 65 L); larger pots reduce drying stress.

source.citation: Utah State University Extension - How to Grow Red Currants in Your Garden (cold hardy to zone 3 or 4; morning sun, afternoon part-shade; air temps above 85 F damage plants; 1 to 2 inches water per week; 1/4 to 1/3 lb of 10-10-10 or 12-12-12 per plant per year; harvest mid-June to mid-July; can be grown in a pot; soil pH ~6.5).
Utah State University Extension - How to Grow Red Currants in Your Garden (watering 1 to 2 inches per week until harvest; drip irrigation due to shallow roots; planting in late fall or early spring).
Utah State University Extension - How to Grow Red Currants in Your Garden (heavy nitrogen feeders; 1/4 to 1/3 lb of 10-10-10 or 12-12-12 fertilizer per plant each year).

University of Minnesota Extension - Growing currants and gooseberries in the home garden (red, pink and white currants are self-fertile; full sun to partial shade, more fruit in full sun; nitrogen-rich composted manure in late fall; common pests aphids, borers, mites, fruit worms; powdery mildew; harvest July-August; plant April-May).
University of Minnesota Extension - Growing currants and gooseberries in the home garden (full sun to partial shade; more fruit in full sun).

Royal Horticultural Society - How to grow redcurrants (grow in a pot at least 45 cm / 18 in wide in John Innes No.3; container plants need consistent moisture all season; feed containers with high-potassium liquid fertiliser every fortnight in the growing season; full sun, tolerates partial shade; harvest from July; can be trained as bush, cordon, fan or standard with supports).

Royal Horticultural Society - How to grow redcurrants (container feeding with high-potassium liquid fertiliser every fortnight during the growing season; ground bushes with annual mulch usually need no feeding).

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Ribes rubrum 'Red Lake' (USDA zones 3 to 7; full sun to part shade with afternoon shade appreciated in hot climates; medium water, avoid overhead watering; flowers April-May, red fruit ripens July; 3 to 5 ft tall; self-fertile; prefers cool summer climates; anthracnose, powdery mildew, leaf spot, currant aphid, scale, mites, fruit flies; alternate host for white pine blister rust).
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Ribes rubrum 'Red Lake' (USDA hardiness zones 3 to 7).

Confidence note: coldHardyMinC set to -34 C as a conservative value for the warmer end of the cited USDA zone 3 range (zone 3b ~ -37 to -34 C); some 'Red Lake'-type cultivars are rated to full zone 3 (~-40 C), so this is intentionally conservative. heatStressC of 29 C derives from USU's '85 F damage' threshold. Container recommendedMinLiters (57 L) is computed from the RHS 45 cm-wide pot guidance at typical bush depth and is an estimate, not a directly published litre figure. wateringProfile baseIntervalDays (3) and hotWeatherMultiplier reflect 'consistent moisture / 1-2 inches per week' for shallow-rooted container culture rather than a published interval.
